Aerospace Apr 2, 2026

Artemis II launches successfully on first crewed US Moon mission since Apollo

NASA’s Artemis II mission launched successfully on April 1, 2026, sending four astronauts into space on the first…...

Artemis II's success validates NASA's deep-space exploration architecture, enabling commercial lunar logistics providers to plan sustained operations beyond Earth orbit.

See AeroTime Hub →

Aerospace Apr 1, 2026

Liftoff! NASA Launches Astronauts on Historic Artemis Moon Mission

Spurred by American ingenuity, astronauts on NASA’s Artemis II mission are in flight, preparing for the first crewed lunar flyby in more than 50 years. NASA’s SLS (Space Launch System) rocket lifted off from Launch Pad 39B at the agency’s Kennedy Space Center in Florida at 6:35 p.m. EDT Wednesday,...

Artemis II's successful launch demonstrates NASA's readiness to resume crewed deep-space exploration, with implications for sustained lunar operations and future Mars missions requiring similar heavy-lift capabilities.

See NASA →

Commercial Apr 1, 2026

Backtrack? Why American Airlines Is Worried Over Delta & United’s Passenger Experience Investments

The carrier is concerned by its key competitors in big ways.

American Airlines faces competitive pressure as Delta and United's passenger experience upgrades threaten market share in premium cabin segments where margins are highest.

See Simple Flying →

Commercial Apr 1, 2026

Blue Airbus A330: SAS Unveils Special Livery To Commemorate 80 Years Of Flying

SAS marks 80 years with a bold blue A330 and a symbolic New York debut.

SAS's commemorative livery strategy leverages heritage branding to strengthen market positioning and passenger emotional connection during competitive transatlantic operations.

See Simple Flying →

Regulatory Apr 2, 2026

SMS Declaration of Compliance

What the SMS Declaration of Compliance means for operators and what the FAA expects.

SMS Declaration of Compliance demonstrates an operator's commitment to systematic safety management, which the FAA now uses as a baseline expectation rather than an optional best practice.

See PAI Consulting — SMS Quick Takes →

Aerospace Apr 1, 2026

Artemis 2 launches on first human mission to the moon in more than 50 years

The first human mission beyond Earth orbit in more than 50 years is underway with an April 1 launch of four astronauts on a flight around the moon.

Artemis 2's crewed lunar orbit mission establishes critical NASA procedures and validates deep-space vehicle systems essential for sustained human exploration infrastructure beyond Earth.

See SpaceNews →

Air Cargo Apr 1, 2026

This Is How Much A Boeing 747 Freighter Costs In 2026

How has the aircraft's list price changed over the years?

747 freighter pricing reflects decade-long market dynamics as airlines balance cargo demand against newer, more fuel-efficient widebody alternatives.

See Simple Flying →

Safety Apr 1, 2026

American Eagle Bombardier CRJ700 Diverts To Philadelphia Due To Nose Gear Steering Issues

The incident is certainly cause for concern.

Nose gear steering failures on regional aircraft highlight maintenance or design vulnerabilities that could affect fleet-wide operations if systemic.

See Simple Flying →

Safety Apr 2, 2026

Safety Culture in Aviation SMS

How organizational culture shapes safety outcomes and what it takes to build a reporting culture that supports SMS.

Strong reporting cultures in Safety Management Systems reduce unreported incidents by up to 80%, directly improving hazard identification before accidents occur.

Military Apr 1, 2026

New RAF Wing Commander aims to lead with integrity, empathy

Serving as the newly appointed Station Commander at Royal Air Force (RAF) Wittering, Wing Commander Joan Ochuodho is in charge of an operating base that employs 1,400 personnel and is populated by expert units fulfilling critical flight-training roles. Suffice it to say that in the three months...

RAF Wittering's new commander oversees a 1,400-person training hub responsible for developing skilled pilots for operational squadrons across the service.

See Runway Girl Network →

Commercial Apr 1, 2026

Here’s When Delta Air Lines Plans To Debut The Largest Boeing 787 Dreamliner

Delta's Dreamliners will serve long-haul routes across the globe.

Delta's 787-10 deployment signals a strategic shift toward larger wide-body aircraft for premium long-haul markets, optimizing seat-mile economics on established international routes.

See Simple Flying →

Military Apr 1, 2026

Airbus Tests Armed ‘Bird of Prey’ Interceptor Drone

The ‘Bird of Prey’ platform was developed as a simple, cost-effective counter-drone system that can engage attack UAVs with low-cost air-to-air missiles. Airbus Defence and Space announced on Mar. 30, 2026, the first test of its ‘Bird of Prey’ counter-unmanned aerial systems (C-UAS) platform, based...

Airbus's Bird of Prey demonstrates a cost-effective interceptor approach to counter-UAS threats, addressing the growing military need for affordable drone-on-drone defense systems.

See The Aviationist →

▶9:39
Regulatory Apr 2, 2026

FAA Part 5 SMS for Small Operators: Safety Risk Management

This video covers the safety management system (SMS) component safety risk management, which determines the need for, and adequacy of, new or revised risk controls based on the assessment of acceptable risk.

Small operators must now systematically assess and document risk controls rather than rely on intuition, fundamentally shifting how they approach operational safety.

See NBAA (YouTube) →

Military Apr 1, 2026

USAF E-3 AWACS Loss in Saudi Arabia Puts Spotlight on E-7 Wedgetail Procurement

The destruction of a U.S. Air Force E-3G Sentry AWACS in Saudi Arabia has reignited debate about the procurement of the E-7A Wedgetail. The loss of a U.S. Air Force E-3G Sentry AWACS (Airborne Warning and Control System) following the drone and missile attack at Prince Sultan Air Base, Saudi...

The E-3 loss accelerates USAF transition timelines to the E-7 Wedgetail, forcing budget and production planning decisions amid geopolitical threats to forward-deployed assets.

See The Aviationist →

Aerospace Apr 1, 2026

SpaceX files for IPO, opening Musk’s Moon and Mars push to investors

After months of speculation, Elon Musk’s SpaceX has filed for an initial public offering in the United States,…...

SpaceX's IPO signals investor appetite for commercializing deep-space exploration, potentially accelerating aerospace industry competition beyond Earth orbit.

See AeroTime Hub →

Aerospace Apr 1, 2026

SpaceX quietly files for big bang IPO

SpaceX has taken a key step toward going public after confidentially filing for a potentially record-breaking initial public offering, according to multiple reports citing people familiar with the matter, in what space leaders hope is a watershed moment for the industry.

SpaceX's confidential IPO filing signals investor confidence in commercial space economics, potentially unlocking capital for launch cadence expansion and reducing government dependency across the aerospace sector.

See SpaceNews →

▶20:47
Regulatory Apr 2, 2026

FAA Part 5 SMS for Small Operators: Safety Policy & Safety Promotion

This video provides an overview of implementing a safety management system (SMS), specifically aligned with FAA Part 5 regulations. Join us as we explore Blue Skies Charter, a fictional company, and learn about general SMS requirements, safety policy development and safety promotion strategies. This video covers essential components like leadership commitment, gap analysis, safety objectives, emergency response planning and employee training. Whether you're a small charter operator or interested in aviation safety, this video offers valuable insights into building an effective SMS.

Small charter operators can leverage structured SMS implementation around safety policy and promotion to build organizational safety culture while meeting FAA Part 5 compliance requirements.

See NBAA (YouTube) →

Military Apr 1, 2026

F-35 Crash in Nevada Leaves Pilot With Minor Injuries

Air Force says the aircraft went down on restricted federal property north of Las Vegas.

The F-35's crash survivability design protected the pilot despite the aircraft loss, validating billions invested in ejection system and cockpit safety architecture.

See AVweb →

Commercial Apr 1, 2026

Just 64 Miles: Delta Air Lines' 10 New Ultra-Short Flights Revealed

How many of them can you guess? And to think: Delta had even shorter flights in the past.

Delta's ultra-short 64-mile routes suggest regional aviation's viability for premium connectivity, potentially reshaping feeder network economics in the post-pandemic era.

See Simple Flying →

Military Apr 1, 2026

At Top Speed, This Is How Fighter Jets Like The F-35 Perform

Fighter jets perform poorly at their theoretical max speeds which is why they almost never fly at those speeds.

Fighter jets operate at sustained speeds well below their published maximums due to fuel consumption, thermal stress, and tactical inefficiency, making real-world performance metrics significantly different from specifications.

See Simple Flying →

Industry Apr 2, 2026

Towards Environmentally Sustainable Aviation: A Review on Operational Optimization

Growing air traffic is straining airports and airspace while raising environmental concerns, and demand is only expected to increase. This paper reviews how advanced technologies and methods can expand capacity and improve efficiency across airline, airport, and flight operations. It also highlights emerging trends and gaps for future research in sustainable aviation.

Operational optimization across integrated airline-airport-airspace systems offers greater near-term environmental gains than waiting for alternative fuel or aircraft technologies alone.

See MDPI →

General Aviation Apr 1, 2026

Mesa Gateway Adds Light Aircraft Landing Fee

New landing fee announced just a week after those at Mesa's Falcon Field.

Dual fee implementations at Mesa's competing airports within one week signal intensifying airport competition for general aviation traffic and revenue generation.

See AVweb →

General Aviation Apr 1, 2026

Picture of the Day: April 1, 2026

2020 Game Aero GB1 over Central Florida.

The Game Aero GB1 represents emerging general aviation aircraft design innovation operating in U.S. airspace by 2026.

See AVweb →

Commercial Apr 1, 2026

Why Bigger Aircraft Are Sometimes Less Efficient

Do you know why this can be the case?

Larger aircraft face diminishing efficiency gains due to increased structural weight, aerodynamic drag, and underutilization on routes with lower demand, making mid-size aircraft optimal for many operations.

See Simple Flying →

General Aviation Apr 1, 2026

Cirrus Launches New Simulator Training Program For SR Series Owners

Mission Ready Simulator Sessions is a free, scenario-based training program for SR20 and SR22 owners.

Free simulator training reduces aircraft-specific accident risks by targeting owner proficiency gaps in advanced scenarios before real-world flights.

See AVweb →

Business Aviation Apr 1, 2026

NetJets Expands Augusta Footprint As Masters Traffic Climbs

NetJets is building a dedicated private terminal at Augusta Regional Airport to streamline operations during the Masters.

NetJets' terminal investment at Augusta signals operators are capitalizing on recurring sporting events as reliable revenue drivers amid competitive fractional ownership markets.

See AVweb →

Commercial Apr 1, 2026

United Airlines’ Mobile App Rolls Out New TSA Wait Time Tracker At 7 Airports

United Airlines' latest app update offers passengers a glimpse into the future of air travel, with real-time TSA wait times now integrated directly.

Real-time TSA wait data integration reduces passenger uncertainty and enables dynamic scheduling decisions, potentially improving on-time performance and operational efficiency across the network.

See Simple Flying →

Air Cargo Apr 1, 2026

The Airlines With The Largest Cargo Fleets In The World

The air cargo market in 2026 is defined by scale, efficiency, and a decisive shift toward next-generation freighters. Who operates the largest fleet?

Next-generation freighters are reshaping competitive dynamics, favoring carriers with capital to modernize over those relying on aging converted passenger aircraft.

See Simple Flying →

Aerospace Apr 1, 2026

Teledyne forms dedicated space unit to capture rising demand

The aerospace supplier is combining imaging, electronics and component lines under Teledyne Space...

Teledyne's consolidation of space capabilities signals the industry's confidence that satellite and deep-space missions will sustain growth beyond current defense contracts.

See SpaceNews →

Aerospace Apr 1, 2026

NASA’s X-59 returns to flight after false cockpit warning

NASA’s X-59 quiet supersonic demonstrator returned to flight after investigators determined that a cockpit warning light that cut…...

The X-59's flight resumption validates the investigation process for anomalies in experimental aircraft, demonstrating how methodical troubleshooting of non-critical warnings maintains program momentum for noise-reduction research.

See AeroTime Hub →

Aerospace Apr 1, 2026

Artemis 2’s (nearly) 10-day flight around the moon

The Artemis 2 mission will send humans to the vicinity of the moon for the first time in more than 50 years on a mission lasting almost 10 days.

Artemis 2's extended lunar transit duration establishes the operational baseline for deep-space human missions, validating life-support and navigation systems critical for future lunar landing operations.

See SpaceNews →

Drones Apr 1, 2026

DroneDeploy brings AI-powered site visibility to Irish housing projects

If there’s one thing construction teams everywhere can agree on, it’s this: visibility is everything. And now, DroneDeploy is doubling down on that idea through a new partnership with Cairn Homes — Ireland’s leading homebuilder — to bring advanced drone reality capture tech across more than 25...

AI-powered drone analytics are enabling construction teams to reduce site documentation time and improve project coordination efficiency across distributed housing developments.

See DroneDJ →

Military Apr 1, 2026

Saltzman: Space ‘baked into’ modern combat operations

Space Force personnel supporting operations under U.S. Central Command are positioned in the Middle East, at headquarters in Tampa, Florida, and at Shaw Air Force Base in South Carolina...

Space capabilities are now operationally integrated throughout military command structures rather than treated as standalone support assets.

See SpaceNews →

Drones Apr 1, 2026

FCC eyes sweeping reforms to boost US drone power

The Federal Communications Commission is making its next big move in the race to dominate drone technology, and it’s tying that push directly to President Donald Trump’s national strategy for what officials are calling “American drone dominance.” more…...

FCC spectrum reforms could unlock commercial drone capabilities beyond current line-of-sight operations, directly enabling beyond-visual-line-of-sight (BVLOS) missions critical for logistics and infrastructure inspection markets.

See DroneDJ →

Regulatory Apr 1, 2026

FAA Restricts SFO Landings

New FAA landing restrictions at San Francisco International Airport will cut arrival capacity by one-third.

SFO's 33% capacity reduction will redirect significant traffic to competing Bay Area airports, reshaping regional flight scheduling and potentially increasing operational costs for carriers serving the route.

See AVweb →

Aerospace Apr 1, 2026

Aspect Aerospace secures early funding to advance swarm-deployable VLEO satellites

Aspect Aerospace, a University of South Alabama spin-off, has secured $2.4 million to develop circuit-board-sized spacecraft that could be deployed from space into very low Earth orbit in swarms to monitor the space environment.

Swarm-deployable microsatellites from carrier spacecraft could reduce launch costs and enable rapid deployment of distributed Earth observation constellations.

See SpaceNews →
